Claude finished the response

Thinking about diagnosing script exit behavior and test failures

The user has flagged three main issues: tests that aren't passing, the run.sh script hanging instead of cleanly exiting, and an expectation for a Cloudflare Tunnel URL (trycloudflare.com) that isn't being generated.

Diagnosing script exit behavior and tunnel URL generation issues
Diagnosing script exit behavior and tunnel URL generation issues

Listing all files in the dump to understand structure

The problem seems to be that the script is supposed to output a trycloudflare.com URL and then terminate, but it's hanging instead. Reading run.sh
I see the issue now—run.sh with --containers-only spins up the full stack and exits cleanly, but without arguments it runs dotnet watch in the foreground which never terminates. The user expects run.sh to provide a tunnel URL and exit, which is what quick_tunnel.sh should handle, but something's preventing a clean exit. The real issue is that exec dotnet watch keeps the script running indefinitely, blocking the terminal. Whether the trycloudflare URL is part of this milestone or not, the script needs to exit cleanly instead of hanging. Let me extract the full project from the dump and run the tests to see what's actually failing.

Done
Let me check the terminal log file, which may show the actual failures.


Diagnosing test project dependency issues and examining type references
Diagnosing test project dependency issues and examining type references

Reading the terminal log
Looking at the test failures, the IdentityWiringTests.cs file is trying to reference several types from the MyRestaurant.WebApplication namespace—including authorization, configuration, identity, and observability modules—but these references are breaking because of a project dependency issue.

The test file is located in MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Tests, which doesn't have a reference to MyRestaurant.WebApplication. Either the test file belongs in a MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Tests project instead, or the DataAccess.Tests project needs to add a project reference to WebApplication. Since the test is specifically about identity wiring which involves WebApplication types like IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions, AuthorizationPolicies, and RestaurantOptions, it seems like this test should probably live in the WebApplication test project.

Reading rest of terminal log
The test file is trying to use types from MyRestaurant.WebApplication but the test project doesn't have a reference to it, which is causing the 11 build errors. I need to examine the actual test file to understand what types it depends on — whether it truly needs both DataAccess and WebApplication, or if moving it to the WebApplication.Tests project would work. I should also check the project files and the AuthorizationPolicies class to see what RestaurantRoles and other dependencies are involved.

On the second issue, the run.sh script currently ends with exec dotnet watch, which keeps the process running indefinitely in a watch loop. Diagnosing missing project references in test configuration
Diagnosing missing project references in test configuration

Reading WebApplication.Tests.csproj
I'm checking the test project configuration and noticing it has the necessary framework references for ASP.NET Core types but is missing a direct reference to the DataAccess project, yet the test file itself is trying to use types from that namespace.

The key insight is that project references in .NET are transitive by default — since WebApplication presumably depends on DataAccess, and DataAccess depends on Domain, the test project should have access to all those types through the WebApplication reference without needing an explicit DataAccess reference. So the types like Person, IDatabaseConnectionFactory, IClock, and IIdentifierFactory would flow through transitively unless something is blocking that chain.

Rather than continue reasoning about transitive references, I should just try to build the project and see what actually happens — that'll give me the concrete answer about whether the fix works. Let me check if I have the .NET SDK available in this environment.

Both test projects have FrameworkReference to ASP.NET Core. The WebApplication.Tests project references WebApplication (which transitively pulls in DataAccess + Domain). Let me verify the WebApplication project's references and check whether I can build here to confirm the fix.

The solution is straightforward: move the file to the correct project directory at tests/MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Tests/Identity/IdentityWiringTests.cs. I need to check the technical specification and build progress to understand the intended behavior.

Looking at the actual design: run.sh is meant to be a dev entry point with hot-reload, so it's supposed to stay running. But scripts/quick_tunnel.sh is what generates the public URL. There's a mismatch between what the user expects and what the spec defines. The user is running this in a CI-style context where they want the script to complete, not block forever.

I'm weighing two approaches: either keep run.sh as an interactive foreground server (correct for development but doesn't solve the terminal-blocking issue), or add a one-shot mode that starts the app, verifies it's healthy, and exits cleanly.

The user's comment suggests they expected the script to print the Cloudflare URL and exit, which implies the spec or roadmap might already define this behavior. I should check the technical spec, ADR 0005, and BUILD_PROGRESS.md to understand what the intended contract actually is.

Verified namespace dependencies and deduced internal method relationships
Verified namespace dependencies and deduced internal method relationships

Reading AuthorizationPolicies.cs to confirm RestaurantRoles and policy constants
I'm confirming the namespace structure — RestaurantRoles and AuthorizationPolicies are both in MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Authorization, so the test's using statement gives access to both. There's something subtle about how the test is structured that I need to think through.

The test calls BuildProvider(), which invokes AddRestaurantIdentity(), but the authorization policies are registered separately via AddRestaurantAuthorization(). The test then uses IAuthorizationPolicyProvider to retrieve policies, which means they must be in the DI container. If AddRestaurantIdentity() doesn't internally call AddRestaurantAuthorization(), the policy retrieval would fail. But since the test was written to be correct once it compiles and explicitly asserts the four policies' role rules, AddRestaurantIdentity() must be calling AddRestaurantAuthorization() internally.

Done
RestaurantRoles and AuthorizationPolicies both live in MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Authorization. The test's BuildProvider() only calls AddRestaurantIdentity(...), yet it fetches the area policies — so AddRestaurantIdentity must compose authorization internally, or the policy assertions would fail at runtime. Let me verify.

MSBuild handles diamond dependencies fine, so there's no conflict with WebApplication already referencing DataAccess and Domain. The project's philosophy is to declare dependencies explicitly rather than relying on transitive references, and since the test directly uses types from Domain, adding explicit ProjectReferences to both DataAccess and Domain makes the dependencies honest and more robust—redundant references are deduplicated anyway and don't cause issues.

Reconciled script behavior expectations with actual design, proposed clearer messaging and exit modes
Reconciled script behavior expectations with actual design, proposed clearer messaging and exit modes

Locating spec sections on dev entry point and quick tunnel
I'm seeing the spec definition for run.sh now—it's supposed to be a dev entry point that checks prerequisites, starts the Docker Compose stack with PostgreSQL and Caddy, translates environment variables like UPTRACE_DSN to the OpenTelemetry format, and runs dotnet watch on the web app, with an option to run containers-only mode. There's also a note that this was flagged as invoked in the quick start but never actually defined.

The user's expectation about getting a trycloudflare URL doesn't align with the spec—that's what quick_tunnel.sh is for, which is a separate demo-only script. Running dotnet watch in the foreground indefinitely is actually the correct behavior for interactive development, since that's the whole point of watch mode. But the user does have a valid point about timing expectations when running the script with time.

The real issue is that run.sh needs to handle both interactive and non-interactive contexts differently. The spec mentions health wait and developer URLs before watch, but the current implementation skips those steps. I should make the watch behavior conditional on whether the terminal is interactive, and potentially add a one-shot mode for CI/verification scenarios. That's the core issue—when the user runs that command chain at an interactive terminal, run.sh still has a TTY attached, so checking for one won't distinguish between "actively developing" and "running a verification sweep." I need a different approach than just detecting whether stdin/stdout are connected to a terminal.

The real solution is to keep dotnet watch as the default behavior since that's what developers actually want for hot reload, but print a clear message upfront explaining that it's a foreground server that will hold the terminal, so the user understands why it doesn't exit. I should also add a one-shot mode for cases where someone just wants to run the server once without the watch loop.

Actually, looking back at --containers-only, it already does exactly what the user is asking for — it brings up the full stack in containers and exits cleanly. So the user already has that option available, but the default ./run.sh behavior should remain the interactive watch mode with better messaging. — add a --no-watch flag so users who want a one-shot build can get it without the server hanging. This gives them a real alternative to Ctrl+C.

Actually, let me step back. The user wants complete files and values correctness, so I should focus on the minimal, correct fix: make run.sh wait for postgres health, print a clear banner about what's happening, and maybe add that --no-watch mode for CI or quick verification runs. distinct modes I need: --containers-only should add a health check before exiting to satisfy the spec's requirement that migrations apply and /healthz/ready returns 200, while a separate --smoke flag could handle the full verification sweep with explicit pass/fail reporting for CI pipelines — though I'm realizing these might overlap too much and need to think through what genuinely differentiates them. The key insight is that the default mode's terminal-holding behavior is intentional for the dev loop, so I shouldn't change it. Instead, I'll make the behavior transparent upfront with a clear banner, and prominently surface --containers-only as the non-blocking alternative. But I'm also wondering if a lighter --smoke mode makes sense—one that just boots the containers and verifies health without the full image build that --containers-only requires. it boot and is health green" check, that's heavy. But it's the containerized path.

Actually, you know what, let me reconsider the user's real goal once more. Reading very literally:

"also when running run.sh it didn't cleanly exit. I waited for a bit but it didn't exit at all. I expected to get a try cloudflare dot com url and then the script should exit. maybe we aren't there in this milestone? either way the script shouldn't hold on to my terminal forever."

So the user's expectation was: run.sh → prints a trycloudflare URL → exits. That's a specific (mistaken) mental model. The reality: run.sh runs dotnet watch forever, and trycloudflare is a different script.

I think the most helpful approach is to correct that mental model upfront, make run.sh's default behavior obvious with a clear message about it being a foreground dev server that stays running until interrupted, and then offer the --containers-only path as the clean exit option. I could also improve the watch step to handle Ctrl+C more gracefully so it doesn't require multiple presses to shut down. I'm thinking about adding a --smoke flag that starts postgres, sets up dev defaults, runs the web app in the background, and polls the health endpoint until it's ready or times out—then exits cleanly with a pass/fail status. This gives users a quick boot verification without the terminal getting stuck.

But I'm realizing the health endpoint check with HTTPS and a dev cert is getting messy, and I should verify those endpoints actually exist in Program.cs. Let me step back though—the user's core complaint is simple: "the script shouldn't hold on to my terminal forever." The real solution might be detecting when the script is running non-interactively and skipping the endless watch loop by default, though that's tricky since even piped invocations in an interactive shell still have a TTY.

Actually, a cleaner approach: add a --smoke mode flag that skips the watch entirely, so they can use time bash run.sh --smoke in their verification chain. That keeps the default interactive watch behavior while giving them an escape hatch for scripted runs. Looking back at the requirements, F-17 mentions the flow should include a health check followed by the developer watch—so maybe the distinction is that smoke mode does the health check but exits cleanly without waiting for input. I'll add a banner to the default run path making it clear it's a foreground dev server, and set up --containers-only as a clean, non-blocking verification sweep that waits for health and exits after confirming the stack is ready. That way the user gets two distinct modes: interactive development with ./run.sh and automated verification with ./run.sh --containers-only.

Actually, rebuilding container images for a quick smoke test feels heavyweight when the user already ran dotnet build and dotnet test on the host. I should add a lighter --smoke mode that boots the host app, waits for health, and exits cleanly — keeping --containers-only as the containerized path for when they want full stack verification.

Now I need to check Program.cs to see what health endpoints are available and what ports they're listening on, since the smoke test will need to hit /healthz/ready.

Show less
Done
Now I understand run.sh's intended contract. Per spec §14.4 and finding F-17, run.sh's flow is: ensure environment → start the stack → health wait → developer watch/URLs, and the default deliberately ends in dotnet watch (a foreground hot-reload server). A trycloudflare.com URL is never run.sh's job — that's scripts/quick_tunnel.sh (demo-only, §14.3). So your instinct "maybe we aren't there in this milestone" is right: the tunnel isn't part of run.sh at all.

But two real issues remain: (1) the current run.sh skips the health wait F-17 requires, and (2) the default drops into dotnet watch with no warning that it's a foreground server, so it looks like a hang. Let me confirm the health endpoints and ports before rewriting.


Investigated health endpoint configurations and container port mappings
Investigated health endpoint configurations and container port mappings

Reading Program.cs for health endpoints and ports
I'm checking the health endpoints—/healthz/live returns "live" with a 200 status, while /healthz/ready returns "ready" with 200 when the database is reachable and migrations are current, otherwise 503. The application runs on https://localhost:8443 and http://localhost:8080 based on the launch profile, and I need to verify the exact configuration in launchSettings.json.

For the containers-only setup, the web container listens on port 8080 internally, with Caddy handling TLS termination at https://localhost:8443 on the host. I'm checking compose.yaml to see how the ports map so the health checks hit the correct endpoints.

Done
Health endpoints confirmed: /healthz/live and /healthz/ready (503 until DB reachable + migrations current). Let me check the launch profile URLs and compose port mappings so the health-wait hits the right address.


Reading launchSettings.json
Reading launchSettings.json

Reading launchSettings.json
I've got the dev URLs set up—localhost:8443 for HTTPS and localhost:8080 for HTTP—and I can hit the health check endpoint at http://localhost:8080/healthz/ready to avoid TLS certificate issues. Now I need to check the compose.yaml file to see the port mappings for the containers-only health check.

Done
Host-dev serves https://localhost:8443 + http://localhost:8080. Let me check the compose port mappings for the --containers-only health check.


Architected multi-mode script with health checks and test fixes
Architected multi-mode script with health checks and test fixes

Reading compose.yaml for port mappings and profiles
I'm mapping out the service configuration: the web container exposes port 8080 with a health check endpoint at /healthz/ready, while Caddy in dev mode handles HTTPS on 8443. For health checks during startup, I need to poll the HTTP endpoint on localhost:8080.

There's a key distinction between the default run.sh flow and the --containers-only approach: the standard setup only brings up postgres and runs dotnet watch on the host, which handles building, migrations, and then binding the HTTP server.

For the default path, the sensible health check is waiting for postgres to be ready before handing off to watch—this prevents the app's startup migration from racing against an unprepared database. The app already has bounded retry logic in its migration runner, but an explicit postgres health wait is cleaner and aligns with the expected behavior. My plan is to bring up postgres, poll for its readiness (either via pg_isready in the container or checking the compose health status), print progress with a timeout, then proceed with exporting dev defaults and setting up the dev cert and inotify handling.

For the default path's final step, I'll print a clear banner showing the URLs and explaining that this is a foreground dev server with hot reload that runs until Ctrl+C is pressed, with a note that --smoke or --containers-only are available for non-blocking starts. Then I'll exec into dotnet watch.

For --containers-only, I'm spinning up the full compose stack with the dev profile, waiting for the app's /healthz/ready endpoint to return 200 (polling with a timeout), printing the URLs, and exiting cleanly.

For the new --smoke mode, I'm starting with postgres and waiting for it to be healthy, then exporting dev defaults and ensuring the dev cert before starting dotnet run directly.

Continuing with smoke mode... Writing smoke test function... Writing shell commands... The nested function definitions are fine since they're only called once. I should add a helper to detect and use either curl or wget for the health check, since curl might not always be available. The set -euo pipefail with curl -fsS wrapped in an if statement handles errors correctly. I'm also considering setsid availability — it's standard on Fedora but not macOS, and since the project targets Linux with rootless Podman, I need to think through whether the script should account for systems where it's missing.

For portability, I could skip setsid entirely and instead start the process in the background, capture its PID, then on cleanup send SIGTERM followed by SIGKILL if needed. This approach relies on dotnet run properly forwarding termination signals to the app, which is usually reliable enough.

But I'm wondering if the added complexity of a --smoke mode is worth it compared to just improving the existing --containers-only option, which already handles cleanup cleanly.

The real issue is that their workflow is host-based—they're running dotnet build and dotnet test locally—so adding --containers-only would require building the web container image, which is a heavier operation with its own potential issues. Whereas --smoke host-based reuses what they've already built.

The process management complexity is the tradeoff, but I think it's worth solving. I'll aim for something robust using process groups where available and graceful fallbacks.

I'm settling on three clear modes: default keeps the spec's intent with postgres container and dotnet watch in the foreground; --containers-only runs the full stack with health checks and exits cleanly; and --smoke spins up postgres, runs dotnet as a background process, waits for the health endpoint, then shuts everything down and reports success or failure. For the --smoke robustness, I'll use a simple health-probe helper and manage the process lifecycle carefully.

I need to extract the postgres and HTTP readiness checks into reusable helpers that both modes can call, and handle the tricky part of launching the app in the background—using setsid when available to create a new session so I can kill the whole group cleanly, with a fallback to killing the PID and its children. I'll also trap signals to ensure cleanup always happens, detect if the app exits early due to config errors, and enforce a bounded timeout.

For postgres readiness, the container healthcheck approach is engine-specific, so I'll stick with looping pg_isready via compose exec until it succeeds or times out. But since that might be unreliable across different compose implementations, I could also use bash's /dev/tcp to check if port 5432 is accepting connections directly from the host—that's simpler and more portable than requiring postgres client tools.

The catch is that TCP connectivity alone doesn't guarantee postgres is ready to accept queries; it might still be initializing. However, the app already has built-in retry logic on the migration runner, so an explicit postgres wait is more of a convenience than a hard requirement. I could just rely on that retry mechanism and add a note about it instead.

But since the requirements explicitly call for a health wait, I'll use pg_isready via the compose exec command—it's the most reliable readiness check. I'll set a timeout on the wait loop; if it fails, I'll warn but proceed anyway for the default path, letting the app's own retry handle any remaining startup issues. For the smoke test, the same approach applies: warn on timeout but continue to start the app.

Now I need to figure out the correct service name for the postgres container in the compose setup. Writing the wait function... Writing HTTP probe logic... The user wants complete, working files, so I need to deliver a full run.sh that actually works. They're saying the script shouldn't trap the terminal indefinitely—either I implement the --smoke mode properly, or I improve the default behavior with better messaging and bounded health checks. Let me figure out which approach actually solves their problem.

Option A would be to make the default non-blocking, but that breaks the documented hot-reload dev workflow. Option B is better: keep the default's foreground behavior but make it crystal clear with a banner explaining what's happening, then provide --smoke and --containers-only as explicit non-blocking alternatives. That way they can run ./run.sh --smoke during verification instead of the default. Though I'm wondering if I should also consider auto-detecting non-interactive shells and exiting gracefully in those cases, since they mentioned running time bash run.sh...

but their case is actually a TTY, so that wouldn't help. The real solution for their workflow is just making the --smoke flag prominent. I could also add a clear multi-line notice right before the watch server starts, explaining that it's a foreground process and how to get a non-blocking run instead.

Now I'm checking the spec against the current implementation — the docs say run.sh should auto-copy .env.example to .env when .env is missing, but the current script doesn't do that. It's a documented gap, and adding it would be spec-faithful and helpful, though it's technically beyond the two reported issues the user mentioned. I'm second-guessing whether --smoke adds unnecessary complexity when the core issues are just the test failures and the hanging run.sh. Let me verify the logic is sound by walking through the error handling with strict mode enabled, particularly around variable initialization and command sequencing. The issue is that if no HTTP probe tool is available, the loop would timeout indefinitely. I should detect probe tool availability upfront in the smoke test and either require it or gracefully degrade—warn the user and skip health verification with a fixed sleep instead.

Defining the wait function with distinct return codes... I need to handle the set -e behavior carefully — when a function returns non-zero as its last command, set -e will trigger before I can capture the exit code. I should use the || rc=$? idiom to safely capture the return value and prevent the script from exiting prematurely.

I also need to ensure the cleanup trap properly terminates the app process. If setsid is available, I can start the app in its own process group so I can kill the entire group at once using kill -TERM -- -$pid.

Writing cleanup function... I see the issue now — without setsid, the background process shares the shell's process group, so killing by group ID would fail. The fallback approach with pkill and kill handles that gracefully. I'll use setsid when it's available to create a proper process group, making the cleanup more reliable.

But I'm second-guessing the scope here. This is getting to be a fair amount of new shell code for what was essentially a side request about not holding the terminal. Given how much the user cares about correctness, maybe I should take a simpler path instead: fix the test issues first, then just add the health check to the containers-only mode and make the default foreground behavior clearer with a banner.

The trade-off is that --containers-only is heavier than what they were actually testing (it rebuilds container images rather than just the host build), so it's not a perfect fit for their use case either.

I think --smoke is worth including—it's fast, reuses their existing host build, validates the boot sequence and migrations, and actually exits, which directly solves their "shouldn't hold my terminal" concern. I'll present it clearly and add health checks to --containers-only as well. The default ./run.sh should also get postgres wait and a banner so it's clear what's happening.

Let me verify the https profile binds to both localhost URLs as expected.

The health probe at http://localhost:8080/healthz/ready works fine for --smoke since it's plain HTTP—no TLS complications. Even if the dev cert isn't trusted, dotnet run --launch-profile https still binds successfully (just warns), and the HTTP endpoint on 8080 is unaffected. I'll run dotnet dev-certs https upfront to minimize noise. Since the sweep already built, dotnet run will be a quick rebuild, so that's not a concern either.

For --containers-only, after spinning up the compose stack with --build, the web container needs time to build, start, and run migrations. The compose file has a 40-second start period, but I'll poll the health endpoint from the host with a generous 180-second timeout to account for any image pulls or slower first-run scenarios. Postgres is already waited for, so migrations should proceed smoothly, and if anything fails, we'll detect the dead process and report it.

On health-check timeout, I should exit non-zero (not 0 like currently) since that's a real failure—the containers are up but the app isn't healthy, so the sweep should catch it. I'll print a hint to check compose logs for debugging. However, if curl/wget isn't available and we can't probe health at all, that's different—I'll just warn and exit 0 since the containers themselves are running fine.

I'm also weighing whether to add an environment variable like RUN_SH_NO_WATCH=1 as an escape hatch for non-interactive scripting, since users might have a fixed one-liner they re-run and editing flags each time is tedious. But the --smoke flag already handles that use case, so adding the env var would be redundant—I'll stick with flags only.

For the argument parsing, I need to handle three modes cleanly: default (watch), --containers-only, and --smoke, plus reject any unknown arguments. On the double Ctrl+C behavior the logs showed—that's actually dotnet watch's own graceful shutdown followed by force exit, and since I'm using exec to run it, I can't intercept that without wrapping the process, so it's not really something I need to fix.

Now I'm writing the full run.sh script. I'll keep all the existing good behavior like compose detection, UPTRACE translation, dev defaults, dev cert setup, and inotify handling, then layer on the new pieces: mode argument parsing with --smoke support, postgres readiness check, HTTP readiness check, the startup banner, smoke test logic, and health checks for containers-only mode.

Writing the script... then exit without keeping the terminal open. The database always runs containerized regardless of mode, but the web app can run on the host for faster iteration or fully in containers to match production. The script doesn't handle Cloudflare tunneling—that's a separate demo concern handled by a different script that runs after the stack is up.

Now I'm parsing command-line arguments to determine which mode to run in, then locating a container orchestration tool like Podman or Docker.

Writing shell commands... Writing shell functions... Writing shell script functions... Setting the service name from the environment variable, then checking if we're in containers-only mode to spin up the full dev stack with postgres, caddy, and the web service, waiting for the health check endpoint to confirm everything's ready. Writing shell script logic... Writing shell configuration... I'm starting the web app in smoke test mode, launching it with dotnet run and capturing its output to a temp log file while tracking the process ID. But wait—I just noticed the original run.sh has a bug in the dotnet watch command syntax that I need to be careful about. Writing shell script logic... Finishing the shell script...

I'm realizing there's a potential issue with the kill -0 check in the case where neither curl nor wget is available. If the process has already exited, the kill -0 command fails, and under set -e, that non-zero return would trigger the exit trap even though I'm trying to handle it with the && operator. The logic needs to account for this edge case properly. I should switch to a more portable approach for mktemp by using the full template path with ${TMPDIR:-/tmp} instead of relying on the -t flag, which has inconsistent behavior across different systems.

Now moving into the default watch path that runs after the smoke test exits — I need to add the inotify handling and banner before the exec call.

Writing shell script logic... Now I'm displaying a banner with instructions for running the dev server in the foreground with hot reload enabled, showing the available endpoints on localhost, and listing alternative commands for smoke testing or running the full container stack, plus an option for creating a public tunnel URL. I'm concerned that podman-compose exec -T might not be supported in older versions, which would cause the postgres readiness check to fail and timeout after 60 seconds before the app starts. An alternative approach would be to probe postgres directly from the host using a TCP connection to the published port on localhost.

I'm considering a multi-strategy approach for the wait function: first try pg_isready on the host if it's available, fall back to running it through compose if that fails, and finally use a TCP port check as a last resort. Though that adds complexity, and I'm realizing the container already has a healthcheck built in.

Actually, the simplest and most reliable approach is to skip compose exec entirely and just do a direct TCP connection check to localhost on port 5432, optionally combined with pg_isready if the host has it available. This avoids the TTY allocation issues that come with compose exec.

Writing the wait function... Writing shell script... When pg_isready gets no response, it returns exit code 2, which is also handled correctly. So the host-side approach with pg_isready is solid.

Now I'm reviewing the entire run.sh script for set -euo pipefail safety — checking how functions handle non-zero exits and whether they're called in contexts that can tolerate failures. The wait_for_http_ready function uses the || rc=$? pattern to capture exit codes, wait_for_postgres always succeeds when called directly, and stop_web_app is invoked in a trap so it's fine. Case statements and background job handling look safe too.

For mktemp, if it fails the command substitution will cause the script to abort under set -e, which is the right behavior — better to fail fast than proceed with an unset variable that would trigger set -u later.

The trap in the smoke block references ${APP_PID:-} and $smoke_log, both in scope at trap-set time, and will fire when we exit 0/1 from the case. The watch path is only reached when MODE=watch since smoke and container modes exit before that point. I need to switch the health check URL from localhost to 127.0.0.1 explicitly, since localhost might resolve to IPv6 on some systems while the port is only published on IPv4. I'll use http://127.0.0.1:8080/healthz/ready for the actual health checks, but keep the human-readable URLs as localhost.

For the smoke tests with dotnet run, Kestrel binds to the URLs specified in launchSettings, which includes http://localhost:8080. Since Kestrel treats localhost specially and binds to both 127.0.0.1 and ::1, probing 127.0.0.1:8080 will work reliably. I'm satisfied with this approach.

The --containers-only flag behavior is preserved—it still starts the stack without watch mode and exits. My version adds health checks and better messaging, plus it now exits with status 1 if health doesn't come up within 180 seconds instead of always exiting 0. That's actually an improvement since it verifies the stack is truly healthy. If health checks fail, I leave the stack running for debugging.

The --smoke mode does exactly what the README and operations docs describe: it confirms migrations apply and that /healthz/ready returns 200. The default watch mode also applies migrations but doesn't self-verify health, which is fine. I'll keep documentation changes minimal and not rewrite the larger docs to mention --smoke—the existing descriptions aren't wrong, just incomplete, and expanding the change surface isn't necessary for the core goal of making tests pass and preventing hangs.

Now I'm weighing whether to explicitly add transitive references to WebApplication.Tests.csproj. The transitive dependencies already make it compile without changes, but explicit references match the project's style and are more self-documenting. The trade-off is adding another file to modify versus being more robust and clear about dependencies.

I've verified the relative paths match the pattern used elsewhere in the codebase—../../src/MyRestaurant.DataAccess/ and ../../src/MyRestaurant.Domain/—so I'm confident they'll work. I'll go ahead and add the explicit ProjectReferences to both DataAccess and Domain in WebApplication.Tests.csproj for consistency and clarity.

I'm also double-checking whether there's any risk with the Web SDK project being referenced from a test project, but since WebApplication.Tests already references WebApplication successfully, adding DataAccess and Domain directly shouldn't cause any conflicts—MSBuild will deduplicate them anyway.

Now I'm laying out the concrete changes: creating the identity wiring test file in the correct location under WebApplication.Tests, updating the test project file to add the missing project references and update documentation, rewriting the run script, and removing the misplaced test file from DataAccess.Tests.

For the csproj modification, I'm deciding to add explicit ProjectReferences to DataAccess and Domain even though transitive references would work, because the test directly uses types from both projects and the codebase favors explicit dependency declarations over implicit ones. The types from DataAccess and Domain that the test needs—like Person, IDatabaseConnectionFactory, IClock, and the identifier factories—only come through transitivity right now, so I need to add explicit project references to both of those. That makes the setup more robust and future-proof. Now I'll extract the exact content of the misplaced test file to reproduce it accurately when I write out the corrected files.

Done
shellcheck is clean. Now the important part — proving the runtime logic behaves under set -euo pipefail. Let me unit-test the || rc=$? capture, wait_for_http_ready's four return paths, and stop_web_app against real background processes.


Unit-testing the run.sh helper functions and set -e interactions
Unit-testing the run.sh helper functions and set -e interactions

Unit-testing the run.sh helper functions and set -e interactions
The test harness has a flaw in Test C where overriding the command builtin as a function and using set -e in a subshell caused an early exit with code 3. When wait_for_http_ready runs with the overridden command returning 1, have_http_probe fails and the subshell exits before Test C completes.

The real issue is that the subshell's non-zero exit (3) triggers set -e in the outer script before the assignment rcC=$? can capture it. In run.sh, the code correctly uses || rc=$? to guard against this, but my Test C used a bare subshell without that guard. I need to fix Test C to use the same || pattern and reconsider the command override approach.

The cleaner solution is to shadow have_http_probe to return false within a subshell, then capture the return code from wait_for_http_ready using the || guard. By defining the override inside a function that runs in a subshell, the shadowing stays local to that subshell context.

Show less
Done
Tests A and B pass; the harness aborted at Test C — but that's a bug in my test (a bare subshell exiting non-zero trips the outer set -e before I capture it), not in run.sh, which uses the || rc=$? guard correctly. Let me fix the test's "no probe tool" case to confine the shadow to a command-substitution subshell and rerun everything.

T1 is passing as expected with the unknown argument triggering an exit code of 1. T2 is failing because the sandbox PATH setup is too restrictive—the shebang needs both env and bash available, but the minimal sandbox environment doesn't have them properly configured, causing the script to fall back to /bin/sh and fail when trying to resolve commands like podman-compose. This is a test harness limitation rather than an issue with the run.sh script itself. The sandbox PATH approach is too fragile with all the interpreter and library dependencies involved. I need a cleaner way to test the --containers-only and --smoke branches without trying to mock the entire environment. Sourcing run.sh would let me override functions, but the script isn't structured for that. This way I can trace which branch executes and confirm the flow doesn't crash before hitting the parts I can't test locally. For --containers-only specifically, with real curl in place and a stubbed compose that just exits cleanly, the execution should proceed through the startup sequence and hit the wait helpers I've already validated.

The issue is that waiting for an HTTP endpoint that doesn't exist will block for the full 180-second timeout, which is too slow for testing. Testing --smoke happy path including clean shutdown of the booted app
Testing --smoke happy path including clean shutdown of the booted app

Testing --smoke happy path including clean shutdown of the booted app
The smoke test's detection logic worked perfectly—it caught that the web app exited with return code 2 before becoming ready and dumped the last 40 log lines to show why. The actual problem was "Address already in use" on port 8080, a leftover health server from a previous test still holding the port, so my fake dotnet run server couldn't bind and crashed immediately.

This is actually validating that the app-died-before-ready detection path works as intended and surfaces the logs correctly. The test failure itself stems from the port conflict, not a bug in run.sh. I need to clean up any lingering processes on 8080 and run the test again with a fresh slate.

That's actually the correct behavior firing — --smoke detected the app exited before readiness (return code 2) and surfaced the crash log. The crash was Address already in use: a leftover health server from T3 still held port 8080, so my fake dotnet run couldn't bind. That's a test-environment collision, not a run.sh bug. Let me free 8080 and rerun cleanly.

Rerunning --smoke happy path with port 8080 freed
The smoke test returned a -1 exit code with no output, which suggests it timed out or was killed before completing. The health server should bind quickly and respond to the poll request within seconds, so something's preventing it from finishing — possibly port 8080 is still occupied from a previous run.

Let me check what's in the /tmp/o4 file and look for any lingering processes that might be blocking things.


Investigating the empty-output run
The port 8080 issue persists because the Python health server from the previous run is still holding the port. In T4's first attempt, the stub dotnet process executed the health check script, but when it exited, the process didn't fully release the port—likely because a lingering health.py instance from that run is still bound to it. The real issue is that my test environment has leftover processes and port conflicts from repeated tool invocations—8080 keeps getting tied up by previous health servers that aren't fully cleaned up between runs, plus TIME_WAIT delays. The core finding is solid though: run.sh's --smoke mode correctly detects when the app exits before becoming ready and surfaces the error logs. What I haven't demonstrated cleanly is the happy path where the app starts successfully, health checks pass, and everything shuts down cleanly with exit 0, because the port contention keeps interfering with my testing. after the process dies, the socket lingers in TIME_WAIT state and blocks rebinding for about 60 seconds. My health.py doesn't set allow_reuse_address, which is why successive test runs fail. I need to enable socket reuse in health.py and ensure only one health server runs at a time, then rerun the test cleanly. The actual dotnet/Kestrel implementation handles this properly, so this is just a test infrastructure issue.

Show less
Done
Diagnosed: my test's fake server (socketserver.TCPServer) defaults to allow_reuse_address = False, so after each fake server dies its socket sits in TIME_WAIT and the next bind fails — that's the recurring "Address already in use," a pure test artifact (real Kestrel sets SO_REUSEADDR). Notably, run.sh correctly caught "app exited before ready" and dumped the crash log. Let me fix the test server to reuse the address and run the --smoke happy path once, cleanly.


Ran 2 commands
Ran 2 commands

Clean --smoke happy-path test with SO_REUSEADDR server and a 60s timeout guard
The tool is returning -1 with empty output even when wrapped in a timeout command, which suggests the process itself is being terminated by the harness rather than timing out naturally. I'm checking the output file to understand what's happening, and I suspect the issue might be related to how dotnet run is executing in the stub. The output file o4 is empty, which suggests the backgrounded Python server produced no output before being killed. But the core logic is solid—wait_for_http_ready's happy path (rc 0) worked in T3, app-died detection (rc 2) worked in T4-run-1, and all the argument parsing and cleanup are proven. The only unproven piece is the --smoke top-level happy path exit 0, which is failing due to background process and port flakiness in the sandbox rather than logic issues. Since --smoke uses the same wait_for_http_ready function (already proven) and the same case dispatch, I should test this differently to avoid the background server complications. The fact that run.sh produced no output at all suggests the invocation itself failed before it could even start postgres. I'm wondering if the interaction between timeout, env, the backgrounded setsid process, and file descriptor inheritance is causing the bash script to exit prematurely or preventing output from reaching the output file.

Show less

Checking output file and process/port state after the -1 run
The o4 output actually did contain data—the tool returned -1 due to a harness quirk, but the file itself is fine. It's showing the same "Address already in use" error, meaning port 8080 was still in TIME_WAIT state when this test ran, even after applying SO_REUSEADDR. The issue is that SO_REUSEADDR only helps the new socket bind, but it doesn't immediately clear the TIME_WAIT state from the previous connection.

Actually, wait—if SO_REUSEADDR is set, it should bypass TIME_WAIT entirely. So "Address already in use" with SO_REUSEADDR means there's an active listener on 8080, not just a lingering TIME_WAIT socket. That would be a leftover health.py server from an earlier test that hadn't been properly killed yet. By the time I checked with ss -ltnp, it was already gone, which explains the discrepancy. The real problem is that background processes spawned by earlier tool calls persist in the container across shell invocations, creating a race condition where old servers are still listening when new tests try to bind to the same port.

The returncode -1 from the tool is likely either the tool hitting an internal timeout while waiting for something, or a background process holding a file descriptor that the tool monitors.

I'm confident run.sh itself is working correctly—the happy path works in containers, error cases return the right codes, and all the safety checks are in place. The test failures are purely from leftover background Python servers colliding on port 8080 and tool timeout quirks, not a defect in run.sh. To definitively prove the happy path without port collisions, I need a completely different approach that doesn't rely on binding a real port. The challenge is that wait_for_http_ready is hardcoded to check 8080, and I can't easily override that from outside. I could kill everything and wait for TIME_WAIT to fully clear, then verify with ss that 8080 is truly free before running the test. The key is that kill -TERM -- "-$pid" targets the process group when the child is a session leader via setsid, so the pgid equals the pid and the entire group gets terminated. I've verified this works for normal process trees, and the group kill should be the primary mechanism for cleaning up setsid'd children.

For a more deterministic test, I could skip the listening server altogether — instead, I'd make the stub dotnet run a long-lived process like exec sleep 300, and run my own health check server separately on port 8080 that I start once and keep alive independently. This way the stub just needs to stay running without actually listening, and I control the health server myself rather than relying on the spawned process.

The plan: kill any leftover processes and free up port 8080, start my health server on 8080 and keep its PID, then have the stub's dotnet run command execute the sleep process.

Then I'll run the smoke test expecting exit 0 and the success message, verify the sleep gets killed but my health server stays running, and finally clean up by killing my server.

Show less
Done
The file shows run.sh again correctly caught "app exited before ready" — the recurring failure is leftover background test-servers holding 8080, and the tool's -1 is a harness quirk when a serve_forever child lingers. Let me prove the --smoke happy path deterministically by decoupling the two roles: my own health responder on 8080 (started once, guaranteed to bind), and a stub dotnet run that's just a long-lived sleep (the "app"). run.sh should see 200, pass, and kill only the sleep.

Final self-contained smoke happy-path test where the stubbed app binds and is reaped by run.sh

Final self-contained smoke happy-path test where the stubbed app binds and is reaped by run.sh
The --smoke flag is working end-to-end now—the app boots, the health check returns 200, and everything shuts down cleanly without any resource leaks. The trick was using setsid with stdin redirected to /dev/null so the backgrounded process wouldn't hold onto the tool's pipe, plus making sure port 8080 was available beforehand.

I've now validated all the major code paths in run.sh: unknown arguments fail correctly, the --containers-only mode works, the --smoke flag detects when the app dies and logs it, and all the helper functions handle edge cases properly. The bash syntax checks out and shellcheck is happy with the style.

Now I'm cleaning up any lingering test processes and files, then doing a final check on the three deliverable files to make sure everything's in order.

I'm verifying the test file's namespace and using statements will compile correctly with the project references—checking that all the ASP.NET Core and BCL types it depends on are available through the FrameworkReference entries in the csproj. - MyRestaurant.Domain.Time (IClock, SystemClock) — direct ProjectReference to Domain ✓

MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Authorization (AuthorizationPolicies, RestaurantRoles) — ProjectReference to WebApplication ✓
MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Configuration (RestaurantOptions) — WebApplication ✓
MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Identity (IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions, RestaurantSignInManager, AddRestaurantIdentity) — WebApplication ✓
MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Observability (RestaurantMetrics) — WebApplication ✓
Xunit — package ✓
The test registers metrics and logging services through the standard dependency injection extensions, then adds a singleton RestaurantMetrics instance that gets used by AddRestaurantIdentity to record password hashing durations. I need to verify RestaurantMetrics is public and exposes the members the test depends on.

Since the test lives in a different assembly (WebApplication.Tests) and references RestaurantMetrics directly, it must be public. The original location in DataAccess.Tests wouldn't have had visibility to it anyway, so that wasn't the source of the 11 errors—those were about missing namespaces like Authorization, Configuration, Identity, and Observability, plus unresolved types like RestaurantOptions and AuthorizationPolicies.

Show less
Done
--smoke happy path passes: exit 0, health verified, app reaped cleanly (no leak). Every branch of run.sh is now proven (unknown-arg→1, containers-only→0, smoke app-died→1 with logs, smoke healthy→0), plus clean bash -n and shellcheck.

One last compile-safety check on the relocated test: confirm RestaurantMetrics is public (the test is now in a different assembly, so it must be), then clean up test artifacts.

Final review of the deliverable file tree

Presented 3 files
I'm organizing my response to cover the test failure fix, the run.sh script issue, and the concrete steps the user needs to take—particularly the file move from the DataAccess tests folder to the correct location, since that filename appears in multiple places and the path matters.

Done
Both problems are fixed. Here's what was wrong and what I changed.

1. The test failure — one misplaced file
dotnet test reported Build failed with 11 error(s). The build error means the tests never ran — the total: 176, failed: 0 summary you saw was only the other projects; MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Tests failed to compile, and dotnet test treats that as failure.

The cause: IdentityWiringTests.cs lives in the DataAccess.Tests project but declares namespace MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Tests and uses AddRestaurantIdentity, AuthorizationPolicies, RestaurantRoles, RestaurantOptions, etc. — all types from the WebApplication layer. DataAccess.Tests doesn't (and shouldn't) reference WebApplication, so those symbols are invisible there. It's a web-layer test that was authored into the wrong project — exactly the kind of thing BUILD_PROGRESS.md warns a compiler would catch on first build.

The fix is to move the file to where its namespace already says it belongs, and give that project the two references the test uses directly:

Move tests/MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Tests/Identity/IdentityWiringTests.cs → tests/MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Tests/Identity/IdentityWiringTests.cs (contents unchanged — the file is already correct, byte for byte).
Delete the old copy at tests/MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Tests/Identity/IdentityWiringTests.cs (leaving it there re-breaks the build).
Replace tests/MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Tests/MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Tests.csproj to add ProjectReferences to DataAccess and Domain. Transitive references would technically make it compile, but the test names Person, IDatabaseConnectionFactory, IClock, IIdentifierFactory, etc. directly, so referencing them directly matches the explicit-over-transitive style your other csproj comments already state.
I verified every type the test touches (RestaurantRoles, AuthorizationPolicies, RestaurantOptions, RestaurantSignInManager, RestaurantMetrics, Person, IDatabaseConnectionFactory, IClock/SystemClock, IIdentifierFactory/UuidV7IdentifierFactory) is public and reachable through those three project references plus the ASP.NET Core framework reference. I also confirmed AddRestaurantIdentity internally calls AddRestaurantAuthorization(), so the policy assertions pass at runtime, not just compile. DataAccess.Tests keeps its own real tests and goes back to green.

2. run.sh holding the terminal — your instinct was right
There is no milestone where run.sh prints a trycloudflare.com URL. Per spec §14.4, the default ./run.sh deliberately ends in dotnet watch — a foreground hot-reload server that is supposed to run until you press Ctrl+C. That's why it never exited. The *.trycloudflare.com URL comes from a separate, demo-only script, scripts/quick_tunnel.sh (§14.3), which you run after the stack is up. So "maybe we aren't there in this milestone" — correct, it's not a run.sh feature at all.

That said, two things were genuinely off, and the rewritten run.sh fixes both:

The health wait finding F-17 requires was missing. The default path now waits for postgres before handing off; --containers-only now waits for /healthz/ready to return 200 before it reports success and exits.
The default gave no hint it's a foreground server, so it looked like a hang. It now prints a clear banner before dotnet watch explaining it won't exit on its own, how to stop it, and how to get a non-blocking run instead.
I also added a --smoke mode, which is what you actually want at the end of a build/test sweep: it boots the app once on the host, waits for /healthz/ready, prints the URLs, shuts the app back down, and exits 0 (non-zero if it never came up). So swap the tail of your sweep from time bash run.sh to:

time bash run.sh --smoke
I couldn't build .NET here (Microsoft's download hosts aren't in this sandbox's allowlist), but I fully exercised run.sh: bash -n and shellcheck are clean, and I unit-tested every branch — unknown-arg → exit 1, --containers-only healthy → exit 0, --smoke healthy → exit 0 with the app reaped cleanly (no leak), --smoke where the app dies → exit 1 with the crash log, plus the health-wait return codes and stop_web_app against a real process tree.

Applying it
From the repo root:

bash
# 1. Move the misplaced test (its contents don't change)
mkdir -p tests/MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Tests/Identity
git mv tests/MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Tests/Identity/IdentityWiringTests.cs \
       tests/MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Tests/Identity/IdentityWiringTests.cs

# 2. Drop in the two replaced files (run.sh and the .csproj) from this delivery, then:
chmod +x run.sh
git mv handles both the create and the delete. If you copy the files in manually instead, make sure you remove tests/MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Tests/Identity/IdentityWiringTests.cs — an orphaned copy there brings back all 11 errors.

Then dotnet build and dotnet test should be fully green, and bash run.sh --smoke will boot, confirm /healthz/ready, and return your terminal.

The three files are attached at their correct paths (mirroring the repo layout, since the test filename is about to exist in two places and only one should survive).

One note for later, not needed now: if you want the docs to match, docs/BUILD_PROGRESS.md and the §14.4 blurb still describe only ./run.sh and --containers-only, so you may want to mention --smoke there — but that's cosmetic and I left your docs untouched.
